sentences.text: My name at birth was Ajzik, A-J-Z-I-K, Rubinstein, R-U-B-I-N-S-T-E-I-N.
I was born on the 8, the 15, 1922.
I had father, mother, and four brothers.
My father's name was Leon Rubinstein.
My mother's name was Cyrla, C-Y-R-L-A.
My oldest brother's name was Morris.
My second brother's name was Myer.
And my third brother's name was David.
My father's occupation was, we had a lumber mill.
A very famous lumber mill in Kielce, Poland.
We live in Kielce, Ulica, U-L-I-C-A, Stolarski, S-T-O-L-A-R-S-K-I, number nine.
It was a Jewish section, yes, and it was a business section.
Mostly, the whole neighborhood was known for lumber mills and woodwork.
I went to school in Kielce, the public school for the first few years.
Later, I went to a-- to a private school.
And I finished public school at the seventh grade.
I went to public school by the name Sinkiwicha, S-I-N-K-I-W-I-C-H-A.
And the private school I went, [?
Yeah, I switched from-- from public school to private school.
And there's times where you're no good, I switch back to public school, and I graduated from public school.
Then I went to work with my father.
Yeah, the private-- the private school was strictly Jewish.
Because my father wants me to have better education.
Was a time where he could afford and was a time where he couldn't afford.
My whole family was strictly Orthodox.
In our city, before the war, we had freedom.
We could go wherever we want.
Wasn't much of a problem before the war, living in Kielce.
Life was usually, I would call it normal.
We had, before the war, anti-Semitism-- anti-Semitism to some degree, but it was livable.
What happened to you, in particular?
Or-- how were you aware of the anti-Semitism?

Time to times, used to be waves, where the Polish people used to write on the walls, [SPEAKING POLISH], 'Jews to Palestine,' or 'Jews to Madagascar.' Used to be different anti-Semitic slogans every few years.
And life went on and on.
Yeah, we had non-Jewish-- mostly our friends were non-Jewish.
But we used to get along pretty good.
Was certain groups of anti-Semite, who used to spread hate time to time.
Less than two, three weeks, blow over, and it was peaceful again.
To some degree, we used to be affected.
But as time went, everything straightens out [INAUDIBLE] and life went on.
Mostly we belonged to Jewish organizations, mostly Orthodox.
We also have Jewish organizations like [INAUDIBLE], Masada, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE].
And the zeal was future to immigrate to Israel.
All organization, we had one zeal.
I belonged to the organization by the name Betar.
It was a right-wing organization, with a zeal, Israel.
Well, we used to have meeting.
People used to speech for us.
We were prepared for the future generation.
The youth, the whole youth, belonged to organization.
My-- my older brothers belong mostly to the same organization.
From an organization you mostly get--
Well, the organization, they build us a future.
They teach us what we should wait for the future.
While we are waiting for the future, in 1939, the war break out where the Nazis marched into Poland.
And in three days' time, they run over the whole country, and occupied Poland.
Well, we had twice a weeks meetings.
I don't remember too much, to be honest with you.
I don't remember too much any more.
More to the right, you know.
We cannot build a future in Poland.
And we have to prepare ourself to leave the country and go build a country, Israel.
But they never gave us the chance to go there.
Well, people, most people used to learn trades, professions, to be prepared to make a living in the future country.
My father in the lumber business, we knew how to manufacture, fabricate lumber.
Dad, and my brothers and the family in the lumber business, right.
Cutting wood, measuring wood, and preparing, you know, the wood, to prefabricate difference things.
No, it's nothing to do with Betar.
They preached the future to immigrate to Israel.
Was because this organization appealed to me.
That we have to immigrate and go build a new land.
Was hard to make a living.
And was hard to survive in a strange land.
We felt like in a strange land.
We'd been living in a strange land.
Was a tough, hard life in a strange land.
We were not treated right there.
Every few years, used to be pogroms on Jews.
Involved my friends, my family, and many, many other people.
The Polish people didn't like the Jewish people for one reason.
The Jewish people were mostly in business and in manufacturing.
And the Polish people never take a part in the economy of the country.
They used to say it every day.
Certain, they used to complain, why the Jews control the factories, why the Jews control the business.
No, no, they didn't say-- Usually, we used to get along fine with the Polish people.
But there were anti-Semite organizations who used to spread up hate, hate against Jew, time to time.
Wave of a anti-Semitic hate they used to spread time to time.
But, you know, the wave passed by, and life went on and on and on.
Certain-- they used to prepare it.
We used to feel that a wave, an anti-Semitic wave would come in time to time.
Yeah, we used to feel it.
Well, used to start off the light way, you know.
We used-- used to go to a football game, you know, a soccer game.
On the way from the soccer game, they used to throw stones at Jews.
They used to beat up a few Jews.
We used to fight back, too.
We used to-- we organized in group.
That's we want to be prepared to fight back.
And we used to beat them up, also.
Betar used to teach us a lot to be defensive, to fight back.
To be strong, united, and fight back.
We used to go in groups.
Five, 10, 20, 30, to be strong and mighty.
Well, used to be a big fight.
We fight it out, and after the fight, police came, straighten out everything, and life went back to normal in a few days.
Police used to treat Jews fair.
The police used to follow the order of the government, yeah.
They didn't stick with the hoodlums.
No, no, I never have any trouble with the police, no, no.
Well, they started it with us.
Mostly going out from a theater, going home from a movie, or going, Saturday and Sunday, to a public park, a state park, on the way home, you know?
After a concert, they used to attack us.
Where the wave of anti-Semite started up.
Used to come and used to go.
We-- we had feeling when starts, when ends.
No, mostly in the city section.
In the park of the city.
Going home from a soccer game.
You have to take the way it came.
We fought back as much as we could.
Usually, people in the city used to get along fine-- Jews, Gentile used to get very fine.
But used to the anti-Semitic groups, who used to stir up the trouble.
But the government didn't support it.
Well, a little bit, the church used to add a little, yeah.
The church bear the most responsibility of the anti-Semitic outbreaks.
Because the church used to spread rumors before every holiday.
They used to spread out rumors against the Jews.
And the ignorant Pollacks used to take it to their heart, and used to attack the Jews.
Mostly before Easter, came out rumors that the Jews kidnap a Christian child, and killed it, and used the blood to bake matzohs for Easter, for Pesach.
Every Easter, we used to have a pogrom.
The Pollacks used to tell us.
They used to tell us, why did you kidnap the Christian child to use the blood?
Was a bunch of ignorant people.
And the people believed them that is truth.
And it church never tried to stop it.
Always we used to expect trouble.
After the rumors, used to come trouble.
Yeah, most the time, used to come trouble.
And we expected for the trouble.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wasn't too much activities in Poland.
Everybody struggled to make a living.
And we worked to make a living, and that's all.
I was working with my father--
And we've been struggling to make a living.
Was very hard to make a living.
Yeah, I did the same activities, like my father.
Yeah, even if I went to school, after school I went to work.
Put in two, three hours' work a day.
Then at night I went with my friends to organizations, to a park, to a movie, to a theater.
Was a city of 80,000 population, and 25% of the population were Jews.
Before the war, I was living in a-- in a poor city.
In the neighborhood, going with the boys, playing football, playing tennis, going skating, going to the park.
We did the best we could find.
How you know that's a change?
Well, in 1939, I think the first week in September, the German armies attacked Poland.
The planes came in and start throwing bombs.
So the start bombing our city.
First we heard on the radio.
Next day, planes came in to our city and throw several bombs.
And before we turned around, on the third days, the Nazi armies marched in and occupied our city.
And after several days, we heard that the whole country was occupied by the Germans.
So it didn't-- so they didn't have much trouble to occupy Poland.
They came in from all around Poland.
